serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Typhoon Shelter Prawns is approximately 300 g, representing a shared plate of 4–6 whole prawns coated in crispy fried garlic, dried chilli, and fermented black bean crumbs.

nutrient_source.title: Prawns, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15150) adapted with frying oil and garlic-breadcrumb coating adjustment
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Typhoon Shelter Prawns exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for cooked shrimp (NDB 15150) with adjustments for deep-frying oil absorption (~8 g fat per 100 g increase), fried garlic and breadcrumb coating (~10 g carbohydrate per 100 g), and fermented black bean seasoning (~300 mg sodium per 100 g addition). Energy recalculated using Atwater factors. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — mixed seafood and protein-dominant dishes with fried coating
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Typhoon Shelter Prawns. GI estimated at 45 based on analogous protein-dominant fried seafood dishes with breadcrumb coating from the University of Sydney GI Database. The high protein and fat content of the dish suppresses glycemic response; the breadcrumb coating contributes moderate carbohydrate. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (45 × 29.4) / 100 ≈ 13.2, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 9.8 × 3 = 29.4 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fermented black beans, douchi seasoning, and added salt
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Typhoon Shelter Prawns due to an estimated sodium content of approximately 520 mg per 100 g (approximately 1,560 mg per restaurant serving), which represents a substantial proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from breadcrumb coating with low glycemic index offset by high protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume Typhoon Shelter Prawns in moderation; the low GI (estimated 45) and high protein content help moderate postprandial glucose response, but the glycemic load per restaurant serving (~13) and the high fat content from deep-frying warrant portion control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated total fat and cholesterol from deep-frying and crustacean content, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Typhoon Shelter Prawns sparingly due to the combination of high sodium (~1,560 mg per serving), elevated cholesterol (~456 mg per serving from prawn content), and significant fat from deep-frying, all of which may adversely affect blood pressure and lipid profiles.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Typhoon Shelter Prawns (避風塘炒蝦, Bì Fēng Táng Chǎo Xiā) is a classic Hong Kong Cantonese seafood dish originating from the typhoon shelter communities of Aberdeen Harbour, where fishing families developed a distinctive cooking style using abundant garlic, dried chilli, fermented black beans (douchi), and crispy breadcrumbs. The dish is prepared by first deep-frying whole head-on prawns until the shells are crisp and golden, then wok-tossing them at high heat with a generous coating of finely minced garlic that has been separately fried until golden and crunchy, along with dried red chillies, fermented black beans, spring onions, and seasoning. The result is an intensely aromatic, savoury, and slightly spicy dish with a satisfying textural contrast between the crispy garlic-breadcrumb crust and the tender, juicy prawn flesh inside. The flavour profile is dominated by deep umami from the fermented black beans, pungent roasted garlic, and a gentle heat from the dried chillies. In Singapore, this dish is served in Chinese seafood restaurants and zi char establishments, often as a premium shared dish. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, moderate-fat food with significant sodium content due to the fermented seasonings and frying process. The dish is not suitable for individuals on sodium-restricted diets and should be consumed in moderation by those managing cardiovascular disease or hypertension. The prawn content provides useful amounts of vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and zinc. The glycemic index is low due to the protein and fat dominance of the dish, making postprandial glucose impact moderate when portion-controlled.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Typhoon Shelter Prawns was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp data with adjustments for deep-frying oil absorption, breadcrumb coating, and fermented black bean seasoning. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for protein-dominant fried seafood dishes. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy (NDB 15150, cooked shrimp) with adjustments for deep-frying oil absorption, garlic-breadcrumb coating, and fermented black bean sodium contribution. No direct laboratory analysis or approved-source entry exists for this specific dish. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy shrimp entry and is not expected to be significant in this preparation. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.1 g per 100 g reflecting trace amounts from frying oil; this is not confirmed zero. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy shrimp data and are approximate. The dish is served at Riverfront Seafood, Boat Quay, Singapore (field survey listing). The food_id GD003333 is from the field survey data seed record. The cuisine_tags in the input included 'Crab' and 'Lobster' which are not applicable to this dish and have been disregarded. Halal status is marked false as fermented black beans (douchi) may contain alcohol from fermentation and the restaurant's halal certification status is unknown.
